customer_name: Wilson Horse and Mule Sale Inc
site_name: Wilson Horse and Mule Sale Inc

citations.narrative:
One adult male common marmoset was accepted for consignment without proof of a health certificate. Health certificates
are important for ensuring that animals are healthy, fit for travel and appear free of any infectious disease or physical
abnormality that would be a danger to themselves, other animals, and the public. A health certificate issued by a licensed
veterinarian, dated not more than 10 days prior to the transportation, must accompany all non-human primates. Correct
from this date forward on Sept. 5, 2025.

citations.desc: Primary enclosures used to transport nonhuman primates.
citations.narrative:
One (1) adult male common marmoset and one (1) juvenile vervet were housed in travel-style pet crates for their transport
enclosures. The openings of the door (approx. 1x1 inch square) and ventilation openings along the sides were big enough
to allow the nonhuman primates to extend their arms and other body parts outside the transport enclosure. While the
vervet had soft mesh placed on the outside of the enclosure, the animal was still able to reach through the openings of the
enclosure and was pulling the mesh inside.
Openings large enough for nonhuman primates to extend parts of its body through can lead to injury of the nonhuman
primates or to other animals or people nearby.
Primary enclosures used to transport nonhuman primates must be constructed so that the nonhuman primates are at all
times securely contained within the enclosure and cannot put any body parts outside the enclosure in a way that could
result in injury to the animals, or to persons or animals nearby.
Correct by: Sept. 8, 2025

citations.desc: Primary enclosures used to transport live animals.
citations.narrative:
Several transport enclosures housing animals did not contain any litter or suitable absorbent material to absorb and cover
excreta. One enclosure housing seven (7) juvenile mountain coatis contained litter but it was soaked, had excreta present
and was sticking to the feet of the coatis and to the enclosure door. One enclosure housing two (2) adult mongoose was
lacking clean litter in sufficient quantity to absorb and cover excreta. There was evidence of liquid on the floor of the
enclosure that had not been absorbed.
Lack of absorbent litter or material to cover excreta and wastes can cause skin/coat contamination, distress, and
increased risk of disease. Animals must be provided with clean litter of a suitable absorbent material that can absorb and
cover excreta.
Correct by: Sept. 8, 2025
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Brittany Kessler, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER
TANESHA WILKINS, SACS n

narrative:
2.78(a) 
   
Health certification and identification. 
One adult male common marmoset was accepted for consignment without proof of a health certificate. Health certificates 
are important for ensuring that animals are healthy, fit for travel and appear free of any infectious disease or physical 
abnormality that would be a danger to themselves, other animals, and the public. A health certificate issued by a licensed 
veterinarian, dated not more than 10 days prior to the transportation, must accompany all non-human primates. Correct 
from this date forward on Sept. 5, 2025. 
3.87(a)(3) 
   
Primary enclosures used to transport nonhuman primates. 
One (1) adult male common marmoset and one (1) juvenile vervet were housed in travel-style pet crates for their transport 
enclosures. The openings of the door (approx. 1x1 inch square) and ventilation openings along the sides were big enough 
to allow the nonhuman primates to extend their arms and other body parts outside the transport enclosure. While the 
vervet had soft mesh placed on the outside of the enclosure, the animal was still able to reach through the openings of the 
enclosure and was pulling the mesh inside.  
 
Openings large enough for nonhuman primates to extend parts of its body through can lead to injury of the nonhuman 
primates or to other animals or people nearby.  
 
Primary enclosures used to transport nonhuman primates must be constructed so that the nonhuman primates are at all 
times securely contained within the enclosure and cannot put any body parts outside the enclosure in a way that could 
result in injury to the animals, or to persons or animals nearby.  
Correct by: Sept. 8, 2025 
3.137(d) 
   
Primary enclosures used to transport live animals. 
Several transport enclosures housing animals did not contain any litter or suitable absorbent material to absorb and cover 
excreta. One enclosure housing seven (7) juvenile mountain coatis contained litter but it was soaked, had excreta present 
and was sticking to the feet of the coatis and to the enclosure door. One enclosure housing two (2) adult mongoose was

lacking clean litter in sufficient quantity to absorb and cover excreta. There was evidence of liquid on the floor of the 
enclosure that had not been absorbed.  
 
Lack of absorbent litter or material to cover excreta and wastes can cause skin/coat contamination, distress, and 
increased risk of disease. Animals must be provided with clean litter of a suitable absorbent material that can absorb and 
cover excreta.  
Correct by: Sept. 8, 2025 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Brittany Kessler, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER 
TANESHA WILKINS, SACS n
